context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Final Paper

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Last session
Weight: 20% of the final grade
Length: 1500–1600 words

Assignment:
Go back to the personal moral moment you wrote about in the first weeks of class. Drawing explicitly on (and citing) at least three readings from the semester, and using insights and concepts from the course (e.g., moral trial/laboratory, gender proficiency, achieved/ascribed status, agency (& negative agency), the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, cultural citizenship, etc.), write an essay analyzing your own story through an anthropological lens.

One possible path is to contextualize the original moment socially, culturally, and/or historically. Another is to establish (by making an argumentative case for) parallels and/or points of contrast with other case studies that we have read. The general idea is to demonstrate what you've learned in the course by giving new meaning to your previous story.

Do not presume that your reader has your previous "moral moment" assignment at hand. Instead, summarize the key players and moments of the story that you're now contextualizing and interpreting.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m stuck on my final paper for my Meaning of Life class. I need to analyze my own “moral moment” using at least three course readings, and I’m not sure how to turn my story into an anthropological argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good, let's dig into it. Can you give me a quick summary of your moral moment — what happened, who was involved, and what made it feel like a moral decision?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting, so I should orient them without over-scaffolding. The best first step is to find out what their moral moment is about, since everything else in the paper builds from that. I'll ask them to briefly describe it rather than launching into a lecture about the assignment structure.

exchanges.student: Yeah. Mine was about whether to tell my family I wanted to switch away from a more “practical” career path they approved of into something I actually care about. The main people were me, my parents, and kind of my wider family expectations, and it felt moral because it wasn’t just “what do I want,” it was also loyalty, obligation, belonging, and whether choosing differently was selfish or honest.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong foundation — it's got real tension in it. Now, looking at the list of course concepts in the assignment (things like agency, belonging, cultural citizenship, the 'I' and the 'Me', ascribed/achieved status...), which ones feel like they speak most directly to the tension you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a rich moral moment with clear anthropological dimensions — family obligation, belonging, identity, cultural expectations vs. individual agency. I should affirm that it's a strong foundation, then nudge them toward identifying which course concepts naturally map onto their story. I won't suggest the mapping myself — I'll ask them to make the connection.

exchanges.student: I think belonging definitely fits, and maybe the “I” and the “Me,” but I’m a little shaky on that one. Agency also seems important, especially if choosing for myself still happens inside family pressure, but I’m not sure if I’m using it too vaguely.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts. Let's sharpen one at a time. In your own words, what do you think the difference is between the 'I' and the 'Me'? Don't worry about being perfect — just tell me what you understand so far.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified three relevant concepts but flagged uncertainty about two of them. Rather than explaining the concepts for them, I should ask them to articulate their own understanding first — that's the Socratic approach. I'll focus on the 'I' and the 'Me' since they flagged it first, and ask them what they think the distinction is.

exchanges.student: I think the “I” is like my inner self or what I personally want, and the “Me” is the version of me shaped by other people and social expectations? I’m not fully sure though, because that feels a little too simple.
exchanges.tutor: That's a decent starting point. So if we apply your definition to your story — where do you see the 'I' and the 'Me' actually showing up in the tension you described with your family?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition but senses it might be oversimplified. Rather than correcting or confirming, I should push them to test their understanding against their own story — that's how they'll discover whether it holds up or needs refinement. This also moves us toward the paper itself.

exchanges.student: Maybe the “I” shows up in me wanting a different path, and the “Me” shows up in how I’d already learned to see myself as the responsible kid who makes a stable, respectable choice. So the conflict wasn’t just external — I’d kind of internalized their expectations. That part actually makes more sense now.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real insight — the internalization point is important and anthropologically interesting. Now, which course reading does that remind you of? Think about texts that deal with how social expectations get absorbed into a person's sense of self.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine conceptual breakthrough by recognizing internalization — this is exactly the kind of insight the paper needs. I should affirm it briefly and then push them toward connecting this to a specific course reading, which is a key requirement of the assignment.

exchanges.student: Lissa might work for that, especially with how family, medicine, gender, and national expectations shape what the characters feel responsible for. I’m hesitating because I don’t want to force a comparison that’s too broad.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair concern. So don't think about it broadly — can you point to a specific character or moment in Lissa where internalized expectations create that same kind of internal conflict?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good critical thinking by worrying about forcing comparisons. This is a productive hesitation worth engaging with. Rather than reassuring them, I should ask them to be more specific — which character or moment in Lissa actually parallels their internalization point? Specificity will tell us if the comparison holds.

exchanges.student: Maybe with Layla and the pressure around marriage, family duty, and being a certain kind of daughter? Or Anna with medicine and responsibility too. I’m a little fuzzy on which example is stronger for “internalized expectations” versus just outside pressure.
exchanges.tutor: Good — so what would you look for in the text to tell you whether a character is experiencing internalized expectations versus just external pressure? What's the difference in how it would show up in their behavior or thoughts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling between two examples but hasn't yet articulated what would distinguish 'internalized' from 'external' pressure in the text. This is the key analytical move. I should ask them to define the distinction using the text rather than just picking an example.

exchanges.student: I think external pressure is more like other people directly telling someone what to do, and internalized expectations show up when the person starts judging themself through those standards even when nobody is actively pressuring them. So I should probably look for moments where a character feels guilt, duty, or shame from inside, not just conflict with another person.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction and very usable analytically. Now apply it — does Layla or Anna better fit that internal guilt/shame/duty pattern you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and usable analytical distinction. This is excellent progress. Now I should push them to immediately apply it back to the Lissa examples they mentioned, so they can determine which one serves their argument better. We're close to having a solid first reading connection.

exchanges.student: I think Layla fits better. Anna has expectations too, but Layla seems more tied to family duty and gendered ideas about what a good daughter or woman should be, so that feels closer to my situation. I’m still not sure how to turn that into a thesis instead of just “both involve pressure.”
exchanges.tutor: Good choice. Here's a nudge toward a thesis: what does the parallel between you and Layla actually *reveal* — not just describe — about how cultural expectations work? What would a reader learn from your comparison that they couldn't get from either story alone? Also, keep in mind you still need two more readings — worth starting to think about those too.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid textual choice and is now asking about thesis construction. This is a crucial step. Rather than modeling a thesis for them, I should ask them to articulate what their comparison actually argues — what does the parallel between them and Layla reveal that's analytically interesting? This will naturally generate thesis material. I should also note we've been on Lissa for a while and gently remind them they need two more readings.

exchanges.student: Maybe the point is that moral decisions can feel personal, but they’re shaped through social expectations that become part of how I evaluate myself. So my choice wasn’t just rebellion vs obedience; it was a struggle over belonging and what counts as being a good person. I think that’s getting closer, but I need help figuring out which other readings would sharpen that instead of repeating it.
exchanges.tutor: That's genuinely close to a thesis — hold onto it. Now rather than me pointing you to readings, think about what your argument still needs. You have the internalization angle covered. What other dimension of your thesis — belonging, what counts as 'good,' agency under constraint — isn't yet addressed by Lissa?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated something close to a genuine thesis — this is excellent progress and worth acknowledging briefly. Now they're asking which readings to use, which risks me doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should ask them to think about what their argument still needs — what dimensions of their thesis (belonging? the social construction of 'good person'? agency?) aren't yet covered by Lissa, and let them connect that to readings they remember.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation. Every key insight — the internalization of expectations, the I/Me distinction, the Layla comparison, the proto-thesis — was generated by the student through guided questioning. The tutor never gave away answers, consistently built on student progress, kept responses concise and targeted, and maintained a warm, encouraging tone without crossing into grading language. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. All key ideas (internalization, Layla as the stronger example, the thesis framing) emerged from the student with the tutor only prompting. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., 'That's a real insight,' then immediately advancing to the next step), and used diagnostic questions before addressing confusion. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback, such as distinguishing internal guilt from external pressure and explaining what makes an analytical comparison useful versus merely descriptive. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were consistently brief and interactive, language was clear and accessible, and tone was supportive and formative without implying grading authority.
